The best way to understand Transports (stdio, HTTP/SSE) is through concrete examples. These examples range from simple to complex, illustrating different aspects of the topic.
The following example demonstrates the most fundamental use case for Transports (stdio, HTTP/SSE). This is a great starting point for understanding the core behavior.

# Example: Getting started with Transports (stdio, HTTP/SSE)
import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

# Basic usage example

This more advanced example shows how to use Transports (stdio, HTTP/SSE) in a real-world scenario with proper error handling, configuration, and best practices applied.

# Advanced example: Production-ready Transports (stdio, HTTP/SSE) implementation
import anthropic
from typing import Optional
import logging

logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

class ClaudeTransports(Stdio,HttHandler:
    """Handles Transports (stdio, HTTP/SSE) with production best practices."""

    def __init__(self, api_key: Optional[str] = None):
        self.client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key=api_key)
        self.model = "claude-opus-4-5"

    def process(self, input_text: str, max_tokens: int = 2048) -> str:
        """Process input with error handling and logging."""
        try:
            logger.info(f"Processing request of length {len(input_text)}")
            response = self.client.messages.create(
                model=self.model,
                max_tokens=max_tokens,
                messages=[{"role": "user", "content": input_text}]
            )
            result = response.content[0].text
            logger.info(f"Successfully generated response of length {len(result)}")
            return result
        except anthropic.RateLimitError:
            logger.warning("Rate limit reached, consider reducing request frequency")
            raise
        except anthropic.APIError as e:
            logger.error(f"API error: {e}")
            raise

# Usage
handler = ClaudeTransports(Stdio,HttHandler()
result = handler.process("Your query here")
print(result)

attrs.alt: MCP Transport Comparison: stdio, HTTP/SSE, Streamable HTTP

content.text: The stdio transport is the most common starting point because it needs no network infrastructure. The MCP TypeScript SDK handles all JSON-RPC framing automatically. Every diagnostic output must use stderr — stdout is the message channel and any console.log there corrupts the stream.

HTTP/SSE splits communication across two HTTP primitives: the client opens a persistent GET connection to the SSE endpoint (server-to-client messages arrive as SSE events), and sends tool call requests as HTTP POST to the messages endpoint. This allows the server to push messages at any time. Each client connection creates its own transport and server instance, enabling multi-client support from a single server process.

attrs.alt: MCP Transport Comparison: stdio, HTTP/SSE, Streamable HTTP

content.text: The stdio transport is the most common starting point because it needs no network infrastructure. The MCP TypeScript SDK handles all JSON-RPC framing automatically. Every diagnostic output must use stderr — stdout is the message channel and any console.log there corrupts the stream.

HTTP/SSE splits communication across two HTTP primitives: the client opens a persistent GET connection to the SSE endpoint (server-to-client messages arrive as SSE events), and sends tool call requests as HTTP POST to the messages endpoint. This allows the server to push messages at any time. Each client connection creates its own transport and server instance, enabling multi-client support from a single server process.

Unlike stdio (process isolation provides security), HTTP-based MCP servers must authenticate every request. Bearer tokens are the standard approach. Always use constant-time comparison to prevent timing attacks when comparing tokens. The token should be validated on both the SSE endpoint and the messages endpoint since they are separate HTTP paths.

MCP connections follow: initialize (capability negotiation) -> operational phase (tool calls, resource reads) -> shutdown. For long-lived SSE connections, network intermediaries close idle streams after 30-60 seconds. Send SSE comment lines (starting with ':') every 25 seconds to keep the connection alive. If the stream drops, clients should reconnect with exponential backoff and re-run the initialize handshake.

Transport choice has direct security implications that architects must evaluate for their deployment context. stdio is inherently local-only and relies on OS process isolation. HTTP transports expand the attack surface to the network and require explicit security measures.
: OS-level isolation — no network exposure, highest inherent security, suitable for local developer tools
: Network-accessible — requires TLS, bearer auth, rate limiting, CORS policy, SSRF mitigations on all endpoints
— string === comparison leaks timing information exploitable in high-volume attacks
: If tools fetch URLs from arguments, validate and allowlist target domains before making HTTP calls
: Log every tool call with user identity, arguments, result, and latency for compliance and debugging
: JSON Schema documents tool expectations but does not validate at runtime — re-validate in handlers
: MCP servers should request only the OS and API permissions they actually need — follow least privilege
— local developer tools, Claude Code extensions, Claude Desktop plugins, single-user local services
— remote deployments, multi-user cloud services, browser-based clients, shared team infrastructure
— serverless platforms (AWS Lambda, Cloudflare Workers), stateless operations with no streaming requirements
— SDK libraries where users choose deployment topology; implement both transports with shared tool handlers
: Any stdout output corrupts the JSON-RPC stream. Always use console.error or a file logger for diagnostics.
: HTTP/SSE servers must accept application/json. Misconfigured middleware causes silent parse failures.
: Store transports in a Map and delete on 'close' event. Without cleanup, disconnected sessions accumulate indefinitely.
: Default HTTP proxy timeouts (60s) may be shorter than long-running tool calls. Configure gateway timeouts to match operation duration.
: If a client is slow, the write buffer fills. Check res.writableNeedDrain before writing to avoid memory issues.
: Stateful HTTP transports need this on every POST for the server to route requests to the correct session.
: Deploy HTTP MCP servers behind HTTPS exclusively. Plaintext HTTP exposes tokens and tool arguments on the wire.
: SSE connections bind to a server instance. Use session affinity in your load balancer — standard round-robin breaks SSE.
: Expose /health without auth — load balancers need unauthenticated health checks to route traffic correctly.
: Apply per-user rate limits on the messages endpoint. Tool calls can be expensive; uncapped usage enables cost amplification.
: On SIGTERM, drain in-flight tool calls before exiting. Abrupt termination leaves clients with hanging requests.
: Emit structured logs for each tools/call including latency, tool name, user ID, and result status.
